class_schedule.topic: Strategic Management Process and Competitive Advantage
learning_objectives: Define strategic management and its components
Distinguish between corporate, business, and functional strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategic management is the integrative capstone of business education. It pulls together marketing, finance, operations, and HR into a coherent plan for creating and sustaining competitive advantage.
key_concepts: Strategy as a theory of how to compete
Vision, mission, and strategic objectives
Competitive advantage: temporary vs. sustained
Strategic management process: analysis, formulation, implementation
Levels of strategy: corporate, business, functional
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing Tesla's strategic evolution from niche EV maker to integrated energy company
setup_requirements: Harvard Business Review case study access

review.value: When a firm earns higher economic value than the average competitor in its industry; sustained when rivals cannot imitate the advantage
Corporate: what industries to compete in. Business: how to compete in each industry. Functional: how each function supports the business strategy

class_schedule.topic: External Analysis: Industry and Competitive Environment
learning_objectives: Apply Porter's Five Forces to assess industry attractiveness
Conduct a PESTEL analysis of the macro-environment
Identify strategic groups within an industry
readings: Porter, The Five Competitive Forces That Shape Strategy (HBR)

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding the external environment is the first step in strategy formulation. Industry structure determines profitability potential, while macro-trends create opportunities and threats.
key_concepts: Porter's Five Forces: rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, buyer power, supplier power
PESTEL: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal
Industry life cycle: introduction, growth, shakeout, maturity, decline
Strategic group analysis and mobility barriers
Competitor analysis: current strategy, objectives, assumptions, capabilities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Five Forces analysis of the streaming entertainment industry, identifying which forces most impact profitability

review.value: The collective strength of the five forces determines industry profitability; strategy should position the firm favorably relative to these forces
Macro-environmental scan of Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal factors affecting all firms in an industry

class_schedule.topic: Internal Analysis: Resources, Capabilities, and Core Competencies
learning_objectives: Apply the VRIO framework to evaluate internal resources
Identify core competencies and dynamic capabilities
readings: Barney, Firm Resources and Sustained Competitive Advantage (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: External analysis tells you where opportunities exist. Internal analysis tells you what you can do about them. The resource-based view argues that unique internal resources are the ultimate source of advantage.
key_concepts: Resource-based view: tangible and intangible resources
VRIO framework: Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, Organized to capture value
Core competencies: activities the firm does better than anyone
Dynamic capabilities: ability to adapt and reconfigure resources
Value chain analysis: primary and support activities
lecture_notes.demonstration: VRIO analysis of Amazon's key resources and capabilities, mapping which provide sustained advantage

review.value: A resource must be Valuable, Rare, costly to Imitate, and the Organization must be able to exploit it to yield sustained competitive advantage
Porter's model of primary activities (inbound logistics through service) and support activities (infrastructure, HR, technology, procurement)

class_schedule.topic: Business-Level Strategy: Cost Leadership and Differentiation
learning_objectives: Compare cost leadership, differentiation, and focus strategies
Analyze the risks of being stuck in the middle
Evaluate blue ocean strategy as an alternative framework

lecture_notes.introduction: Business-level strategy defines how a firm competes within a particular industry. Porter's generic strategies remain the foundational framework, though newer approaches challenge his either/or dichotomy.
key_concepts: Cost leadership: economies of scale, learning curve, tight cost control
Differentiation: unique value through quality, innovation, brand, service
Focus strategy: targeting a narrow segment with cost or differentiation
Stuck in the middle: failing to commit to either strategy
Blue ocean: creating new market space rather than competing in existing markets
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing business-level strategies of Walmart (cost leadership), Apple (differentiation), and Trader Joe's (focused differentiation)

review.value: Achieving the lowest cost position in the industry through scale, efficiency, and cost control; competes on price
Creating uncontested market space by simultaneously pursuing differentiation and low cost, making competition irrelevant

learning_objectives: Evaluate related and unrelated diversification strategies
Apply the BCG matrix and GE-McKinsey matrix
Analyze strategic alliances and their governance structures

lecture_notes.introduction: Corporate strategy addresses the scope of the firm: which businesses to be in, how to allocate resources across them, and how to create synergies that make the whole worth more than the sum of parts.
key_concepts: Related diversification: leveraging shared resources and capabilities
Unrelated diversification: financial synergies and risk reduction
BCG matrix: stars, cash cows, question marks, dogs
Strategic alliances: joint ventures, licensing, equity partnerships
Make-or-buy-or-ally decision framework
lecture_notes.demonstration: BCG matrix analysis of Alphabet (Google) portfolio: Search, Cloud, YouTube, Waymo, Other Bets

review.value: Entering new businesses that share resources with existing ones; creates value through economies of scope and knowledge transfer
Classifies business units by market growth rate and relative market share to guide resource allocation decisions

learning_objectives: Design control systems that support strategy execution
Manage strategic change and overcome resistance

lecture_notes.introduction: A brilliant strategy poorly executed is worth nothing. Implementation is where strategy meets reality, and it requires aligning structure, culture, people, and systems.
key_concepts: Strategy-structure fit: matching organizational design to strategy
Balanced scorecard for strategy execution tracking
Corporate culture as strategy enabler or barrier
Kotter's 8-step change model
Strategic leadership and the role of the CEO
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study of Microsoft's cultural transformation under Satya Nadella: from 'know-it-all' to 'learn-it-all'

review.value: Functional structure for single-business, multidivisional for diversified, matrix for multiple priorities; misalignment undermines execution
Create urgency, build coalition, form vision, communicate, empower action, generate wins, consolidate, anchor in culture

questions.question: Using Porter's Five Forces, explain why the airline industry has historically been less profitable than the software industry, citing at least one specific force for each.
questions.answer: Airlines face intense rivalry (many competitors, undifferentiated product, high fixed costs leading to price wars), high buyer power (price-sensitive customers with easy comparison shopping), and low barriers to substitutes (trains, cars, video conferencing for business travel). Software firms enjoy high barriers to entry (network effects, switching costs), low buyer power (customers locked into ecosystems), and weak substitutes. The collective force pressure is much greater in airlines, driving down industry profitability.

rubric.key: At least one force analyzed for airlines
At least one force analyzed for software
Clear connection between forces and profitability

questions.question: Apply the VRIO framework to evaluate whether Coca-Cola's brand constitutes a sustained competitive advantage. Assess each criterion.
questions.answer: Valuable: Yes, the brand drives premium pricing, customer loyalty, and distribution access worth billions in revenue. Rare: Yes, no other beverage brand has comparable global recognition and emotional connection. Inimitable: Yes, brand equity was built over 130+ years through cumulative advertising, cultural embedding, and consumer nostalgia; competitors cannot replicate this history. Organized: Yes, Coca-Cola invests heavily in brand management, marketing, and legal protection to exploit the brand. Since all four VRIO criteria are met, the brand is a source of sustained competitive advantage.

rubric.key: Correctly assesses all four VRIO criteria
Reaches correct conclusion about sustained advantage

questions.question: A mid-size hotel chain is considering two strategies: (A) cutting costs by 20% through automation and standardization, or (B) investing in luxury upgrades to justify premium pricing. What additional information would you need to recommend one over the other?
questions.answer: Key information needed: (1) Current competitive position and target market segments, (2) competitor strategies and industry profitability drivers, (3) customer willingness to pay for luxury features vs. price sensitivity, (4) internal capabilities for each approach (operational efficiency expertise vs. luxury hospitality talent), (5) capital requirements and financial capacity for each investment, (6) market trends regarding travel preferences. The choice depends on where the company can build a defensible position; being stuck in the middle with a half-hearted version of either would be the worst outcome.

rubric.key: Identifies at least three relevant information needs
Links information needs to strategic decision factors

questions.question: Amazon has diversified from online retail into cloud computing (AWS), streaming (Prime Video), grocery (Whole Foods), and healthcare. Is this related or unrelated diversification? Explain the strategic logic.
questions.answer: This is primarily related diversification. The strategic logic is shared capabilities: AWS leverages Amazon's massive computing infrastructure built for retail; Prime Video drives Prime membership which increases retail spending; Whole Foods integrates with Amazon's logistics and delivery infrastructure; healthcare leverages data analytics and distribution capabilities. The common thread is Amazon's core competencies in logistics, technology infrastructure, data analytics, and customer-centric platform design. Each business benefits from and strengthens these shared resources, creating economies of scope.

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Strategic Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a publicly traded company and conduct a full strategic analysis including external analysis (PESTEL, Five Forces), internal analysis (VRIO, value chain), strategy identification, and strategic recommendations. Present findings as a professional strategy consulting report.
objectives: Apply the complete strategic analysis framework to a real company
Synthesize external and internal analyses into strategic insights
Present complex analysis in a clear, executive-level format
deliverables: External analysis: PESTEL and Five Forces with supporting data
Internal analysis: VRIO assessment and value chain analysis

criteria: Five Forces analysis with evidence for each force
Key opportunities and threats clearly identified
VRIO assessment of key resources with justification
Value chain analysis identifies competitive advantages
Implementation roadmap is realistic and detailed
All claims supported by evidence and citations
Rigorous external and internal analysis with evidence

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of strategic analysis frameworks and application

case_discussions.description: Quality of contributions to in-class case study discussions

midterm_examination.description: Covers strategic analysis frameworks and business-level strategy

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with emphasis on corporate strategy and implementation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance mandatory for case discussions. Students must read cases before class and come prepared to contribute.
course_policies.late_submissions: Strategic analysis report penalized 10% per day. No late submissions accepted for case discussion preparation.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual strategic analysis. Case discussion preparation may involve study groups but in-class contributions are individually assessed.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analysis must be original. Citing analyst reports is acceptable; copying their analysis or using AI to generate analysis is not.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to financial databases (Bloomberg, S&P Capital IQ) through university library. Presentation software.
